title: uasync
html:
uasync

Initialize the Uniface application to receive asynchronous messages.

int uasync (HANDLER handler );

Parameters

handler
Address of the function that will handle the asynchronous messages. The handler function should have return value int and two parameters of type unsigned char* and short. The value returned by the handler is immaterial; it is not used. Passing a zero value (null pointer) causes the default handler to be used.

Return Values

Table 1. Values returned by uasync
Value Meaning
0 Successful completion
-3 Could not create the handler thread
-5 The message queue could not be created or opened
-7 Could not get the current process ID

Use

Unix platforms only (except Tru64 Unix). Library: yrtl.

Description

uasync initializes a Uniface application to receive asynchronous messages.

Note that in Uniface V8, calling uasync is no longer a requirement to receive asynchronous messages. By using the assignment setting $IPC_ASYNC, you can set up the application to receive messages through a default handler, which consists of passing the message on to the application’s receiveMessage trigger. You only use uasync if you want to specify a non-default handler. It is allowed to use the assignment setting, as well as to call uasync, as shown below.

When you first call uasync, the following occurs:

  • An IPC message queue is created with the key equal to the current process ID.
  • A handler thread is created that reads from this queue and passes the messages to the kernel.
  • The kernel is instructed to call the specified handler function whenever a message is received, passing the message and its length to the handler as parameters.

The handler function is fully responsible for handling the message. When the message needs to be sent to the application’s receiveMessage trigger, the handler must explicitly do so by calling uputames.

Subsequent calls to uasync will omit steps 1 and 2, and serve only to change the handler function. When the assignment setting $IPC_ASYNC is used, the queue and thread are created at startup, using a default handler. Subsequent calls to uasync will have the effect only of changing the handler function.

Because of the C-specific interface of uasync (using a function pointer parameter), uasync cannot be called using a C signature. It must be called from a user 3GL routine that is typically performed or activated in the apStart trigger.

Example: uasync and unoasync

The following 3GL code defines a handler that passes the message to the application’s receiveMessage trigger, as well as logging an informational message in the syslog file (this particular action is specific to Unix). The application would perform or activate ASYNC_INIT at the start of the application’s receiveMessage trigger.

#include <h3gl.h>
#include <syslog.h>

int HANDLER (unsigned char* msg, short len)
{
char buf[500];
strncpy(buf, msg, len); /* Copy the message */
buf[len] = 0; /* Add the zero terminator */
uputames((unsigned char *)buf, len);/* Pass message to ASYNC trigger */
syslog(LOG_INFO, buf); /* Write message to system log file */
return 1; /* Return any value (it is not used) */
}

int ASYNC_INIT ()
{ return uasync(HANDLER); }
int ASYNC_DEINIT ()
{ return unoasync(); }
Related Links
  • uasymsg

    Send an asynchronous message to a local Uniface application.

  • unoasync

    Disable the handling of asynchronous messages by the application.

  • uputames

    Put an asynchronous message in the input queue of the calling application.

  • $IPC_ASYNC

    Create a thread that listens for interprocess communication (IPC) messages.
